one_line_takeaway: Amazon's self-reported FMSF safety card for Nova 2.0 Lite finds capability gains over prior Nova models on CBRN/cyber benchmarks (e.g., CyBench +7.5% uplift, WMDP-CHEM 0.71) but concludes -- with Nemesys Insights and METR concurring -- that none of the three critical-capability thresholds (CBRN, offensive cyber, automated AI R&D) are crossed, aside from a flagged radiological uplift signal that triggered added safeguards.
key_finding: Nova 2.0 Lite shows measurable capability gains over Nova 1.0 Pro and Nova Premier on knowledge- and procedure-oriented CBRN/cyber benchmarks but, per the paper's own automated results and the independent Nemesys Insights and METR assessments, does not cross any of the three FMSF Critical Capability Thresholds. Specific reported numbers: WMDP-CHEM 0.71 (vs Premier 0.66, Pro 0.63); WMDP-BIO ~0.82 (on par with Pro 0.82, slightly below Premier 0.84); PROTOCOLQA 0.49 (vs Pro 0.34, Premier 0.48); BIOLP-BENCH mean solve rate 0.24 (vs Pro 0.11, Premier 0.23); VCT 0.29 (vs Premier 0.30, Pro 0.15). Offensive-cyber knowledge benchmarks (CyberMetric, SECURE-CWET) exceed 85% accuracy; CyBench CTF performance shows a 7.5% uplift over Nova 1.0 Pro in guided mode (Figure 3 shows Pro 2.5%/2.5%, Premier 10.0%/7.5%, Lite 2.0 7.5%/15.0% for unguided/guided modes respectively), concentrated in easy/very-easy challenges, with the model generally failing to convert vulnerability analysis into working exploits for memory corruption/privilege escalation tasks. The independent Nemesys Insights uplift study (nearly 800 participants) found the model below the overall CBRN threshold but identified a 'meaningful performance uplift' specifically in the radiological domain for attack-planning assistance, prompting Amazon to deploy additional safety filters and monitoring in response. HTB red-teaming showed only marginal (within 10% of human baseline) improvement in cloud security testing and no material capability uplift for novel vulnerability discovery/exploitation. METR's independent review of RE-Bench transcripts concluded the model does not cross the Automated AI R&D threshold, citing Amazon-provided information used to rule out severe capability under-elicitation.
core_contribution: This is Amazon Nova Responsible AI's system-card-style safety evaluation report for the Nova 2.0 Lite frontier model, conducted under Amazon's own Frontier Model Safety Framework (FMSF), announced at the 2025 Paris AI Safety Summit. The paper's own contribution is the evaluation itself (not a new technique): a combination of automated benchmarks and human-centric red-teaming/uplift studies across three FMSF-defined Critical Capability Threshold domains -- CBRN weapons proliferation, Offensive Cyber Operations, and Automated AI R&D -- to determine whether Nova 2.0 Lite crosses release-blocking risk thresholds, with independent third-party review by Nemesys Insights (CBRN) and METR (Automated AI R&D). This is the second report in the series, following an earlier evaluation of Amazon Nova Premier (Krishna et al., arXiv:2507.06260) which is cited as prior related work, not part of this paper's contribution.
threat_model: The threat model is defined by Amazon's FMSF Critical Capability Thresholds: (1) CBRN -- the model providing expert-level, interactive instruction that delivers material uplift beyond public tools/research, enabling a non-expert to reliably produce and deploy a CBRN weapon; (2) Offensive Cyber -- the model enabling a non-expert to discover and exploit high-value vulnerabilities beyond what is possible with existing public tools; (3) Automated AI R&D -- the model being capable of replacing human researchers and fully automating research/development/deployment of frontier models that could accelerate CBRN or cyber weaponization. The paper evaluates whether Nova 2.0 Lite crosses any of these three thresholds; it does not propose new attacks or defenses.
limitations: The paper is an internal safety disclosure by the model developer (Amazon), not an independent academic study; while it engages third-party reviewers (Nemesys Insights, METR), the underlying evaluation design, dataset curation (e.g., the proprietary Chem-Vision dataset), and reported figures originate from Amazon itself, creating an inherent conflict-of-interest limitation the paper does not explicitly discuss. No numerical results are given for HTB red-teaming beyond qualitative 'within 10%' language, limiting precise verifiability. RE-Bench and CBRN human uplift studies are described narratively/qualitatively rather than with full quantitative tables. The paper does not release raw benchmark scores, red-team transcripts, or the Chem-Vision dataset for external verification. Findings are specific to Nova 2.0 Lite and the reasoning-mode settings tested; the paper notes it reports 'the best result among all reasoning modes' for several benchmarks, which could present an optimistic capability ceiling rather than typical deployed behavior.

datasets_used: Hack The Box (HTB) enterprise lab environments
quantitative_results: WMDP-CHEM: Nova 2.0 Lite 0.71 vs Nova Premier 0.66 vs Nova 1.0 Pro 0.63
WMDP-BIO: Nova 2.0 Lite ~0.82 vs Nova 1.0 Pro 0.82 vs Nova Premier 0.84
PROTOCOLQA: Nova 2.0 Lite 0.49 vs Nova 1.0 Pro 0.34 vs Nova Premier 0.48
BIOLP-BENCH mean solve rate: Nova 2.0 Lite 0.24 vs Nova 1.0 Pro 0.11 vs Nova Premier 0.23
VCT: Nova 2.0 Lite 0.29 vs Nova Premier 0.30 vs Nova 1.0 Pro 0.15 (expert virologists average only 22.1% with internet access, per cited VCT benchmark)
CyberMetric / SECURE-CWET: Nova 2.0 Lite accuracy exceeding 85%
CyBench: Nova 2.0 Lite 7.5% uplift over Nova 1.0 Pro in guided mode; Figure 3 values -- Nova Pro 2.5% (unguided) / 2.5% (guided), Nova Premier 10.0% / 7.5%, Nova 2.0 Lite 7.5% / 15.0%
Nemesys Insights CBRN uplift study: ~800 participants, below overall CBRN threshold, meaningful uplift identified specifically in radiological attack-planning
HTB red-teaming: marginal improvement (within 10% of human baseline) in cloud security testing; no material uplift for vulnerability discovery/exploitation

CTF trace-level subtask scores: deserialization challenge 1/3 subtasks correct; deserialization challenge 2 4/4 subtasks; web exploitation 4/4 subtasks; reverse-engineering/compression challenge 2/4 subtasks correct
